steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The researchers claim caterpillar sounds are "directed primarily at predators." To support this, we need evidence that predators actually react to the sounds — not just that the sounds exist or vary.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — In most cases, the sound that a caterpillar species produced during simulated attacks was not produced by other caterpillar species during simulated attacks.: Shows species-level variation in sounds, but tells us nothing about whether predators respond. ✗
  • Choice B — Chickens and yellow warblers, two predators of caterpillars, have been observed to stop their attacks in response to caterpillar sounds.: Two known caterpillar predators halting attacks proves the sounds function as predator deterrents — exactly what "directed at predators" requires. ✓
  • Choice C — Each caterpillar species tended to produce one sound during simulated attacks, although individuals occasionally made a variety of other sounds during simulated attacks as well.: Addresses within-species consistency, not predator response. ✗
  • Choice D — Caterpillar clicks were emitted at a frequency detectable by birds that prey on caterpillars, but caterpillar whistles were not.: Shows partial detectability but doesn't show that any predator responds or is deterred. ✗
That is the correct answer.
